title: Adaptive SLO Shepherding & Capacity Fencing Playbook
preview:
Overview

This playbook codifies an adaptive approach we call "SLO shepherding" for day-to-day SRE operations: proactively protecting critical SLOs by applying capacity fencing, tenant reservations, and temporary degradation policies while preserving error-budget fairness across tenants. It combines measurement, aut...
text:
Overview

This playbook codifies an adaptive approach we call "SLO shepherding" for day-to-day SRE operations: proactively protecting critical SLOs by applying capacity fencing, tenant reservations, and temporary degradation policies while preserving error-budget fairness across tenants. It combines measurement, automated tooling, and human guardrails to avoid surprise outages and prevent long reconciliations after incidents.

Scope & applicability

- Applies to multi-tenant inference services: inference-gateway, model-host-pool, and routing-proxy.
- Intended for services with latency or availability SLOs and where capacity is a constrained resource (GPU pools, KV cache memory, or token throughput).
- Not intended for non-production dev environments or single-tenant dedicated customers (those use dedicated capacity contracts).

Goals

1. Keep critical customer-facing SLOs within targe...

title: SRE Prioritized Risk Remediation Framework
preview:
Overview

This document defines a prioritized risk remediation framework that ties SLOs, error budgets, capacity planning, incident reviews, and operational tooling into a single, repeatable remediation lifecycle for Redwood Inference services. It is intended for SREs, service owners, and platform engineers ...
text:
Overview
========
This document defines a prioritized risk remediation framework that ties SLOs, error budgets, capacity planning, incident reviews, and operational tooling into a single, repeatable remediation lifecycle for Redwood Inference services. It is intended for SREs, service owners, and platform engineers who are responsible for reducing systemic risk across model-serving and control-plane services.

Goals
-----
- Make remediation decisions deterministic and measurable.
- Prioritize work that yields the largest reduction in customer-visible risk per engineering-hour.
- Ensure remediation actions are linked to SLO outcomes and error-budget burn signals.
- Embed remediation into regular reliability initiatives (quarterly guardrails, sprint work, and on-call action items).

Scope
-----
Includes: model-serving frontends, batch embedding pipelines, autoscaling controllers, cache ...

preview:

It combines manual escalation, deterministic...
text:
Summary
-------
This runbook describes an operational pattern and step-by-step procedure for re-allocating SLO budget and rapidly provisioning or shifting capacity to hot execution paths ("hot-path" services) during acute traffic events that threaten customer-facing SLOs. It combines manual escalation, deterministic automation gates, and verification checks so that SREs and paged responders can act quickly with low risk.

Scope and motivation
--------------------
- Applies to Redwood services that touch real-time inference and request-routing: inference-gateway, request-router, kv-cache, continuous-batcher, model-worker, and capacity-allocator.
- Use when an SLO is projecting to miss within the current burn window due to sudden load, model regression, or regional capacity loss.
- Goal: restore SLO headroom to a safe state within 10–30 minutes for high-severity incidents while preservi...

Overview:

This runbook describes Redwood's standardized procedure for applying predictive capacity throttling and performing a controlled canary rollback when early signals indicate SLO risk. It codifies the decision thresholds, required telemetry, automation hooks, and human escalation steps so teams can reduce blast radius while preserving customer-facing availability.

Purpose and scope:
- Purpose: minimize customer impact by automatically and safely mitigating traffic surges or degraded model performance before error budgets are exhausted.
- Scope: applies to public inference endpoints, Dedicated pools, and Private VPC deployments where automated routing and throttle policies are enabled. Does NOT cover manual DBA/infra maintenance windows.

Key definitions:

title: Automated Customer Incident Reporting and SLO Impact Generator
preview:

This document defines the design, operational runbook, verification checks, and customer-facing templates for Redwood's Automated Customer Incident Reporting and SLO Impact Generator (ACIR-SIG). text:
Summary:

This document defines the design, operational runbook, verification checks, and customer-facing templates for Redwood's Automated Customer Incident Reporting and SLO Impact Generator (ACIR-SIG). ACIR-SIG produces standardized, auditable customer incident summaries within a defined SLA window after an incident is classified as customer-impacting. The system computes SLO impact automatically from telemetry and timeline evidence, assembles a concise narrative, and supports automated delivery to customers (via Console, email, or support ticket) with human-in-the-loop gating.

Goals and scope:
- Goal: Reduce time-to-customer-summary while preserving accuracy, traceability, and blameless language.
- Scope: infra and inference incidents that affect production API responses (latency, availability, errors). Excludes: billing, account-level policy, or non-production incidents.
- Outpu...

text:
Overview

Purpose: This page describes the standard approach for designing, running, and closing SRE incident rehearsals (tabletops and simulated drills) that validate SLO-driven response, capacity continuity, error‑budget governance, and operational tooling readiness. It codifies roles, cadence, success criteria, metrics to collect, and post‑exercise artifacts so rehearsals are repeatable and measurable.

Why this matters
- Regular, well-facilitated rehearsals shorten time-to-recovery in real incidents, improve cross-team coordination, and surface capacity or telemetry gaps before customer impact.
- Tying each rehearsal to specific SLOs and error‑budget states lets us prioritize exercises that protect customer-facing guarantees.

Scope and audience
- Scope: multi-region production service incidents, capacity surges, model-serving regressions, and control-plane outages that affect inf...

Overview:

This runbook defines the Tenant Impact Scorer (TIS), a deterministic scoring algorithm and automation flow that:
- Quantifies incident impact at the tenant/customer level.
- Converts impact into a prioritized remediation level (P0..P3).
- Drives automated gating of non-critical deployments and throttles via the gatekeeper-service when error budgets or capacity signals cross thresholds.

Goal:
- Reduce time-to-prioritize for multi-tenant incidents.
- Ensure capacity/economics aware mitigation (avoid global throttles for a small set of high-error tenants).
- Provide a reproducible signal for oncall and product owners to take action.

Scope:
- Applies to all customer-facing inference endpoints and long-running background jobs that surface tenant errors (service prefixes: inference-api/*, embeddings/*, reranking/*).

- Integrate incident review evidence with capacity remediation tickets and follow-up actions.
- Provide runbooks and tooling references to reduce triage time and avoid ad-hoc mitigations.

title: SLO Safeguard & Canary Governance Playbook

text:
Overview:
This playbook defines governance, operational controls, and tooling patterns for running SLO-focused experiments and model canaries on Redwood's inference fleet. It establishes a safety-first approach so teams can iterate model rollouts and runtime tuning while protecting customer-facing SLOs and error budgets.

Scope:
- Applies to any production-facing model rollout, quantization change, kernel-switch, or runtime configuration that can alter latency, tail latency, or correctness metrics.
- Intended audience: SREs, Service Stewards, Model Owners, Capacity Engineers, Release Engineers.

Definitions:
- SLO (Service Level Objective): the target level of reliability (example: p99 latency & availability).
- Error Budget: allowable SLO violation over a window (30d default).
- Canary: a staged rollout (traffic percentage + time) used to validate changes.
- Safety Net: automated and...

preview:

text:
Overview

This page defines a reproducible classifier that maps SLO signals to a customer-impact tier and a prescriptive remediation workflow. It is intended to: align SRE, Product, and Customer Success during SLO degradations; minimize time-to-customer-remediation; and create consistent inputs for post-incident reviews and error-budget decisions.

Audience
- On-call SREs
- SRE leads and managers
- Incident commanders (ICs)
- Customer Success (CS) and Support for high-impact incidents

Principles
1) Tie every SLO alert to an explicit customer-impact tier (1–4).
2) Automate low-friction routing and remediation for common degradations.
3) Escalate proactively for any Tier 1/2 customer impact.
4) Preserve evidence for postmortems and error-budget reconciliations.

Definitions
- SLO: Service Level Objective (e.g., request_latency_p95 <= 200ms).
- Error budget: 1 - SLO target over the defi...

title: Service Stability: Financial Friction & Capacity Trading Manual
preview:
Overview

This manual defines the operational and financial mechanism we use to reconcile SLO-driven error budgets, cross-team capacity needs, and short-term capacity trading. The goal is to reduce friction for business-driven launches while protecting global availability and preserving fair allocation across tenant...
text:
Overview

This manual defines the operational and financial mechanism we use to reconcile SLO-driven error budgets, cross-team capacity needs, and short-term capacity trading. The goal is to reduce friction for business-driven launches while protecting global availability and preserving fair allocation across tenants and services.

Who should read this
- SREs owning model-serving SLOs
- Product/feature owners planning launches
- Capacity planners and finance partners
- Service owners who may request temporary headroom

Key terms
- SLO: Service Level Objective (e.g. 99.5% p99 latency for text-generation).
- Error budget: allowable SLO budget (1 - SLO) consumed by poor performance.
- Capacity credit: a time-bounded token issued to a team permitting additional headroom above nominal allocation.
- Trading window: the period (default 72 hours) during which capacity credits can be used.

Pr...

title: capacity-signal-fabric-and-incident-fuse-playbook-2026
preview:
Overview

This playbook defines the "Capacity Signal Fabric" and the complementary "Incident Fuse" pattern for Redwood Inference service teams. The intent is to unify signals (SLOs, capacity telemetry, cost and model-level health) into a single, operational fabric that can drive deterministic hedging decisions, auto...
text:
Overview

This playbook defines the "Capacity Signal Fabric" and the complementary "Incident Fuse" pattern for Redwood Inference service teams. The intent is to unify signals (SLOs, capacity telemetry, cost and model-level health) into a single, operational fabric that can drive deterministic hedging decisions, automated mitigations, and incident fuse behavior when global or cross-service capacity risk is detected. The document covers signal taxonomy, thresholds, automation runbooks, incident review checklist, and recommended tooling integrations.

Scope

- Applies to all model-serving and routing services in the hosted and dedicated tiers.
- Focus areas: SLOs & error budgets, capacity planning hedges, reliability initiatives, incident fuse triggers and automated mitigations, runbooks for responders, and post-incident standards.
- Not covered: billing reconciliation, long-term contrac...

title: Runbook: Audit Log Pipeline Incident Response (Ingestion + Export)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook covers detection, triage, mitigation, and customer communications for incidents affecting audit log **ingestion**, **processing/normalization**, and **export** (SIEM targets) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

Audit logs are a security control: we optimize for durability and ...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook covers detection, triage, mitigation, and customer communications for incidents affecting audit log **ingestion**, **processing/normalization**, and **export** (SIEM targets) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

Audit logs are a security control: we optimize for durability and correctness over perfect exactly-once semantics. Our default delivery goal is **at-least-once**; duplicates are possible and must be handled via `event_id` by consumers.

## Scope
In-scope components (names may differ slightly by region/environment):
- **Producers**: API Gateway, Control Plane (tenant/config/policy/RBAC), Console backend, Private installer/upgrade tooling
- **Audit ingest**: `audit-ingest` (HTTP/gRPC) + authn/authz for producers
- **Buffer**: Kafka topic(s) `audit.events.v1` / `audit.events.dlq` (Hosted/Dedicated)
- **Processor/normalizer**: `audit-normaliz...

title: 2026 H1 Reliability Initiative: Auto-rollback on SLO Breach for Rollouts
preview:
## Summary
This 2026 H1 Reliability Initiative hardens Redwood’s rollout safety rails by wiring rollout automation (canary + A/B + staged deployments) to SLO monitors and enabling automatic mitigation (pause and/or rollback) on verified SLO breaches.

The goal is to materially reduce customer impact and on-call load...
text:
## Summary
This 2026 H1 Reliability Initiative hardens Redwood’s rollout safety rails by wiring rollout automation (canary + A/B + staged deployments) to SLO monitors and enabling automatic mitigation (pause and/or rollback) on verified SLO breaches.

The goal is to materially reduce customer impact and on-call load from a recurring incident class: latency and error regressions introduced during rollouts (model version changes, routing policy changes, runtime config changes).

## Motivation / Why this matters
Redwood’s differentiation depends on predictable latency and reliability across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. Over the last two quarters, the fastest path to customer-impacting incidents has been: 
- a rollout increases tail latency (p95/p99) or 5xx/timeout rates,
- the regression is not noticed quickly enough (or is attributed incorrectly),
- mitigation is manual a...

title: Kernel Auto-Selection v2 — SLO Impact Assessment (Runtime 1.21 Rollout)
preview:
## Summary
Kernel Auto-Selection v2 ("KASv2") changes the kernel choice logic in the Serving Runtime based on model family/architecture, sequence-length regime (prefill/decode characteristics), and GPU SKU. The change is intended to reduce p95/p99 latency and stabilize throughput across heterogeneous fleets (A100/H1...
text:
## Summary
Kernel Auto-Selection v2 ("KASv2") changes the kernel choice logic in the Serving Runtime based on model family/architecture, sequence-length regime (prefill/decode characteristics), and GPU SKU. The change is intended to reduce p95/p99 latency and stabilize throughput across heterogeneous fleets (A100/H100/L40S), but it introduces a new failure surface: mis-selection (wrong kernel for a workload or SKU), tail-latency spikes on long-context prefill, and telemetry-driven alert noise.

This SRE assessment defines:
- Projected SLO impact (positive/negative risks) and what we will measure to validate.
- Canary criteria and stop/rollback triggers.
- Alert adjustments required for safe staging and to prevent false positives.
- Error-budget policy during rollout (Hosted first, then Dedicated pools).

**Decision:** Proceed with staged rollout behind feature gates with strict regres...

text:
## Purpose
This page defines how Redwood’s **incident taxonomy** is used to:
1) map incidents to the right **SLOs** (availability vs. latency vs. streaming) and corresponding **error budgets**, and
2) produce consistent reporting that breaks down error-budget burn by **incident_type** and **owner**.

This is part of the Reliability Program goal to reduce incident frequency and improve MTTR via clearer ownership, consistent classification, and better tooling.

---

## Scope
**Applies to:** Hosted API, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

**In-scope incident classes (taxonomy v1):**
- Availability (5xx/overload)
- Latency regressions
- Streaming disconnects
- Routing/fallback misbehavior
- Capacity/fleet shortages
- Control plane outages
- Auth/RBAC failures
- Telemetry/metrics blind spots
- Private deployment connectivity/config issues

**Out of scope on this page:** deep root-cause an...

preview:

This page defines Redwood Inference’s canonical **error budget policy** for tiered SLOs (Hosted and Dedicated) and how we **enforce** it via burn-rate alerting, incident thresholds, mitigation expectations, and release/deploy gates.

This policy exists to:

text:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s canonical **error budget policy** for tiered SLOs (Hosted and Dedicated) and how we **enforce** it via burn-rate alerting, incident thresholds, mitigation expectations, and release/deploy gates.

This policy exists to:
- Make SLO adherence measurable and comparable across teams and regions.
- Create predictable operational behavior during reliability regressions (latency spikes, 5xx bursts, stream disconnects).
- Align Product SLAs/expectations with engineering measurement and operational controls.

## Scope
**In scope (Q1 2026 program phase):**
- Tiers: **Hosted (shared)**, **Dedicated (reserved capacity)**
- Services/paths: API Gateway, Routing/Control Plane policy evaluation, model serving request path, telemetry needed to compute SLIs
- SLIs: success rate (non-5xx), latency percentiles, streaming reliability (disconnect/error events...

title: Runbook: Optimize Quality Gate Failures (triage, mitigation, rollback, escalation)
preview:

This runbook covers operational response when **Optimize quality gates** fail, flap, or time out. It is intended for SRE oncall and incident commanders during beta/limited production rollout.

Quality gates are mandatory evaluation checks required before applying Optimize recommendations (batching, prefix...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook covers operational response when **Optimize quality gates** fail, flap, or time out. It is intended for SRE oncall and incident commanders during beta/limited production rollout.

Quality gates are mandatory evaluation checks required before applying Optimize recommendations (batching, prefix/KV caching, quantization profile changes) to production traffic.

This runbook focuses on:
- Triage steps (what failed, where, why)
- Distinguishing **real quality regressions** vs **eval flakiness / infrastructure issues**
- Safe mitigations (pause/rollback/disable) and when to use overrides
- Escalation paths and required artifacts

## When to use this runbook
Use this runbook when any of the following occurs:
- A production or staging rollout is **blocked** due to `quality_gate_failed`.
- Gate run pass rate drops sharply (potential system issue or a regression).
- Gate ...

title: SLO definitions for rollout safety (auto-pause/auto-rollback signals)
preview:
## Purpose
This document defines which Service Level Objectives (SLOs) are eligible to act as *safety signals* for Redwood rollouts (canary, A/B, staged model deployments) and provides recommended thresholds by customer tier.

These SLOs are consumed by the rollout controller’s SLO evaluator to decide whether to **p...
text:
## Purpose
This document defines which Service Level Objectives (SLOs) are eligible to act as *safety signals* for Redwood rollouts (canary, A/B, staged model deployments) and provides recommended thresholds by customer tier.

These SLOs are consumed by the rollout controller’s SLO evaluator to decide whether to **pause** a rollout or **rollback** traffic to the last known-good baseline.

## Scope
Applies to:
- Hosted API routes (multi-tenant)
- Dedicated capacity routes
- Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments where Redwood operates/observes the serving plane

Out of scope for initial release:
- Quality/regression eval signals (handled by eval/guardrails)
- Multi-region failover (beyond rollback/pause)

## Terminology
- **Rollout scope**: the specific tuple the evaluator queries against: *(service/route, model version, region, customer tier)*.
- **Baseline**: currently promoted/last-known...

title: Per-model latency SLOs and alerting policy (perf canary)
preview:

It maps canary signals to SRE-style concepts (SLOs, error budgets, paging hygiene), establishes **warning vs paging** thresholds, and documents **notification routing and owner...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood’s alerting policy for **per-model performance regressions** detected by the production **perf-canary**. It maps canary signals to SRE-style concepts (SLOs, error budgets, paging hygiene), establishes **warning vs paging** thresholds, and documents **notification routing and ownership**.

This is intentionally *not* a customer-facing SLA document. It is an internal policy for protecting production serving performance and keeping on-call load manageable.

## Scope
In scope:
- Perf-canary synthetic workload measurements per model (and optionally per region/tier/SKU as configured).
- Alerting for **p95 latency regressions** and **tokens/sec drops**, plus attribution breakouts (queueing vs runtime execution).
- Routing rules for #eng-runtime, #eng-oncall, and incident-bot.

Out of scope:
- Customer prompt shadowing or any alerting tied to customer conte...

title: SDK client retry impact analysis (thundering herd risk) + rollout monitoring
preview:
## Executive summary
Client retries are a double-edged sword: they reduce tail latency for transient failures, but can amplify load during partial outages ("thundering herd"), worsening the incident and extending recovery.

For the SDK parity initiative, SRE reviewed the proposed default retry policy and modeled loa...
text:
## Executive summary
Client retries are a double-edged sword: they reduce tail latency for transient failures, but can amplify load during partial outages ("thundering herd"), worsening the incident and extending recovery.

For the SDK parity initiative, SRE reviewed the proposed default retry policy and modeled load amplification under several failure modes (429 rate limiting, 5xx service errors, and network instability). The conclusion is that **the standardized retry defaults are safe** *if* we:

1) **Respect `Retry-After` on 429**, 2) apply **exponential backoff with jitter**, 3) keep a **tight max attempt budget**, and 4) **do not automatically retry streaming mid-flight**.

This page documents the analysis, recommended guardrails, and the dashboards/alerts required for rollout.

---

## Background and why this matters
### What changed
Across Python/TypeScript/Go, we are standard...

title: Cost Model API — SLOs, SLIs, and Error Budgets

text:
## Purpose
This document defines the Service Level Objectives (SLOs), Service Level Indicators (SLIs), and error budget policy for the **Cost Model API** (service name: `cost-model-api`). It covers:
- Availability and latency targets for production traffic
- A correctness/drift SLI to ensure cost outputs remain finance-usable and product-consistent
- Alerting and escalation policy

The Cost Model API is a shared dependency for **Redwood Console** cost dashboards and **Redwood Optimize** ROI/what-if projections. The SLOs below are designed to keep Console usable on hot paths while providing guardrails for cost drift that can impact customer trust and finance reporting.

## Service summary
**What it does:** Computes standardized cost primitives and breakdowns (prefill/decode, cache attribution, batch efficiency, overhead allocation) and supports what-if projections.

**Primary endpoints...

Usage Insights is user-facing and interactive. It relies on upstream aggregation jobs and a query service. Because the feature is used for spen...
text:
## Overview
This page defines the service-level objectives (SLOs), error budget policy, and performance budget for the **Usage Insights** experience in the Redwood Console.

Usage Insights is user-facing and interactive. It relies on upstream aggregation jobs and a query service. Because the feature is used for spend attribution and financial workflows, we treat **correctness and availability** as first-order goals and we explicitly document what we will and will not guarantee in Beta vs GA.

## In-scope components
**User journeys covered by SLOs**
- Interactive breakdown queries in Console (tables and drill-downs)
 - Cost/usage by **route + model** (optionally filtered by workspace/project, region, deployment mode)
 - “Top cost drivers” and “What changed?” summaries
 - Data freshness banner retrieval
- API-backed retrieval used by Console (same endpoints)

**Components included in th...

title: Tiered SLOs + Error Budget Program (Q1 2026)
preview:
## Overview

text:
## Overview
This program formalizes Redwood Inference’s tiered reliability commitments by defining canonical SLIs/SLOs for **Hosted (shared)** vs **Dedicated (reserved capacity)**, and adopting a consistent, enforced **error budget policy** that connects reliability outcomes to operational behavior (alerting, incident triggers, and release controls).

This page is the Q1 2026 program overview (motivation, scope, milestones, owners, dependencies, and success metrics). Detailed definitions and policy are maintained in the linked pages under **SLOs & Error Budgets**.

---

## Why we are doing this (motivation)
Customer escalations in late 2025 and early 2026 highlighted recurring gaps:
- **Latency spikes during burst traffic** (especially p95/p99) causing customer-visible degradation and unpredictable throughput.
- Elevated **5xx rates** and **stream disconnects** during rollouts and/or ...

text:
Overview

This page defines the standardized SLO Binding Contract between service/product teams and SRE for model-inference services, and documents a practical set of proactive capacity heuristics SRE should apply during onboarding, steady-state operations, and incident remediation. The intent is to make the negotiation explicit, reduce revision cycles, and provide reproducible capacity calculations for inference workloads (generation, chat, embeddings, reranking).

Why this exists

- Inference workloads have multi-dimensional resource patterns (tokens/sec, sequence length, KV cache longevity, batchability). Generic capacity rules lead to overprovisioning or SLO regressions.
- We need a reproducible contract so product teams understand the operational impact of SLOs (cost + availability) and SRE has a clear set of inputs for capacity planning and runbook actions.

Scope

- Applies to ...

- Ensure paging triggers correlate to real customer impact (not only internal “red” alerts).

Overview
========

Short intro: This field guide documents standard operating patterns for containing SLO burn and reshaping traffic during multi-tenant LLM inference incidents. It is aimed at on-call SREs, platform engineers, and owners of tenant-facing routing policies. Readable end-to-end: detection -> immediate containment -> recovery -> post-incident actions.

Scope and intent
----------------
- Scope: Redwood hosted & Dedicated inference control plane and routing layer (traffic-proxy, admission-controller, and model-router).
- Intent: Give deterministic, low-risk steps to:
 - stop or slow SLO degradation in-flight,
 - preserve high-priority tenant commitments, and
 - buy time for capacity/provisioning or rollback.
- Out of scope: model code changes and downstream customer app fixes. Those are handled via product triage.

## Summary
This page defines production SLOs, error budgets, and safety guardrails for the Shadow Traffic capability (mirroring sampled production requests to shadow variants and scoring outputs asynchronously). The primary reliability requirement is **fail-open** behavior: user traffic must not be degraded by shadowing failures.

This document covers:
- Overhead budgets for the user-serving (control) path
- Acceptable error rates/latency for the shadow path and scoring pipeline
- Alert thresholds (page vs ticket) and required dashboards
- Guardrails (circuit breakers, backpressure, cost caps)

Applies to: Hosted API, Dedicated, and Private deployments (with local equivalents for Private).

text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to triage, validate, and mitigate alerts emitted by **perf-canary** for per-model performance regressions:
- **p95 latency** increase (end-to-end and/or runtime-only)
- **tokens/sec** decrease

The goal is to:
1) quickly verify the alert is real (not noise/cardinality/baseline drift),
2) localize the regression (model vs region vs hardware vs tier),
3) attribute likely cause (kernel vs scheduler vs model config vs infra/fleet),
4) take the lowest-risk mitigation (pause rollout, rollback, drain nodes, disable a kernel, etc.),
5) route escalation to the right team with the right artifacts.

perf-canary uses **synthetic prompt shapes** (no customer prompts) and should remain under the **GPU overhead budget**. Do not attempt to “increase canary load” during incident response without SRE/runtime approval.

---

## When this runbook applies
You are in t...

title: Hosted API tail-latency guardrails for KV cache compaction/eviction rollout
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines SRE-facing guardrails to protect Hosted API tail latency (p95/p99) during the staged rollout of KV cache compaction + tier-aware eviction in the serving runtime.

The rollout introduces new runtime behaviors (budgeted compaction work and eviction under pressure) that can improve long-tai...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines SRE-facing guardrails to protect Hosted API tail latency (p95/p99) during the staged rollout of KV cache compaction + tier-aware eviction in the serving runtime.

The rollout introduces new runtime behaviors (budgeted compaction work and eviction under pressure) that can improve long-tail performance under bursty mixed-length decode workloads, but can also regress latency if:
- compaction work is performed on the critical path more than intended,
- eviction selection becomes too aggressive for certain traffic mixes,
- allocator state oscillates (fragmentation ↔ compaction ↔ eviction) and increases CPU/GPU overhead,
- cancellation/streaming edge cases increase lock contention.

This page standardizes:
- **SLO targets** and what counts as a rollback-worthy regression
- **Alert thresholds** and paging policy during the canary
- **Automated rollback criteria**...

## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s **multi-region availability SLOs** for 2026, covering:

- **Dedicated** (reserved capacity, tenant-isolated)

The intent is to:

## Purpose
This runbook describes the on-call procedure for executing (or approving) **Hosted API** regional failover during a real regional impairment or a controlled test. It covers triage, validation of trigger signals, initiating automation (including dry-run), monitoring during traffic shift, rollback, and post-event verification.

**Primary goal:** reduce customer-visible impact by failing over quickly when the region is actually impaired, while avoiding false positives and oscillation.

## Audience
- SRE On-Call (primary)
- Routing/Smart Routing On-Call (secondary)
- Network On-Call (as needed)
- Control Plane On-Call (as needed)

## Definitions
- **Primary region:** currently preferred region for a Hosted API route/model group (e.g., `us-east` for most NA traffic).
- **Failover:** shifting eligible traffic from an impaired region to one or more healthy regions via routing poli...

preview:

This page describes the design, configuration, and operational runbook for the Progressive Capacity Rebalancer (PCR) - a safety-first autoscaling orchestration component Redwood SRE uses to rebalance reserved GPU capacity across model tenants when traffic surges, error budgets deplete, or regional capacity ...
text:
Summary:
This page describes the design, configuration, and operational runbook for the Progressive Capacity Rebalancer (PCR) - a safety-first autoscaling orchestration component Redwood SRE uses to rebalance reserved GPU capacity across model tenants when traffic surges, error budgets deplete, or regional capacity events occur. The PCR complements existing autoscalers by applying SLO-aware, staged reallocation and graceful drain semantics to minimize customer impact during capacity shocks.

Goals:
- Preserve customer-facing SLOs (p99 latency and availability targets) for critical tenants while minimizing global request loss.
- Apply predictable, auditable capacity shifts with automatic rollback on SLO regressions.
- Provide an operator-run, testable mechanism for rapid capacity rebalancing during incidents and planned maintenance.

When to use PCR:
- Multi-tenant cluster shortage lea...

title: Incident Retrospectives, Error-Budget Governance, and Capacity Ownership Playbook
preview:

It is intended for SREs, serving-runtime engineers, platform owners, and oncall engineers who participate in incident response and reliability initiati...
text:
Summary

This playbook defines a single, pragmatic process that ties SLOs and error budgets to capacity planning, incident review standards, and operational tooling. It is intended for SREs, serving-runtime engineers, platform owners, and oncall engineers who participate in incident response and reliability initiatives.

Scope

- Applies to all customer-facing inference services (hosted API, Dedicated clusters) and the routing/control plane that affects request admission or fallbacks.
- Excludes non-production test clusters unless an incident impacts production via shared infra.

Principles

1. Make decisions from measurable signals (SLOs, queue depth, GPU metrics, and latency histograms).
2. Prioritize fast guardrails over perfect prediction — we want safe defaults and clear escalation.
3. Keep reviews blameless, actionable, and fast: initial incident timeline within 72 hours, RCA wi...

title: Regional Failover Automation Upgrade — Program Overview (Reliability Program 2026)
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Inference is upgrading its regional failover automation as part of the 2026 Reliability Program (SLO maturity, incident reduction, and graceful degradation).

Today, region failover is a mix of manual steps and partially automated routing policies. Trigger signals are frequently noisy (false posit...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Inference is upgrading its regional failover automation as part of the 2026 Reliability Program (SLO maturity, incident reduction, and graceful degradation).

Today, region failover is a mix of manual steps and partially automated routing policies. Trigger signals are frequently noisy (false positives), slow to confirm true regional impairment, and hard to validate safely. During real incidents, this increases customer-visible errors, creates capacity cascades, and increases on-call cognitive load.

This initiative standardizes trigger signal hierarchy, adds deterministic policy evaluation and rollback guardrails, improves observability, and validates the end-to-end system via controlled failover tests (“game days”) for Hosted API and Dedicated.

---

## Problem statement
### Customer impact we are addressing
When a region is impaired (network reachability, elevated...

Summary
-------
This playbook documents the SLO Impact Simulator and Capacity Sandbox ("SISCS"), a reproducible environment and procedure for validating SLO changes, error-budget policies, autoscaling rules, and fallback strategies before applying them to production. SISCS is a low-risk way to estimate user-facing availability, latency, and cost impacts across traffic patterns and capacity tiers.

Goals
-----
- Provide an executable sandbox to simulate traffic, faults, and routing policies against a production-like stack.
- Quantify SLO and error budget outcomes for proposed changes (SLO tightening, burst handling, reduced reserved capacity).
- Surface regressions in observability, alerting, and oncall playbooks before rollout.
- Produce reproducible experiment artifacts and a clear decision log for stakeholders.

Scope and non-goals
-------------------
- In-scope: model-serving stack...

text:
Overview

This playbook codifies a recurring, cross-team canary rehearsal and escalation tabletop exercise designed to validate: SLO behavior during partial rollouts, automated and manual rollback paths, paging and escalation flows, and end-to-end telemetry (synthetic and production). The goal is to surface gaps in runbooks, alerting, and team coordination before a real incident. Exercises should be scheduled quarterly for each high-impact service and after major changes to routing, autoscaling, or rollout tooling.

Scope

- Services: model-serving frontends, request-router (smart-router), autoscaler, cache layer (prefix/KV), billing-rate-limiters.
- Failure modes simulated: degraded tail latency, model-variant regression, cache-priming failure, burst-induced overload, and partial config rollouts.
- Not in scope: full data-plane outages requiring cloud provider escalation; these are c...

This document defines the SLO Contract Exchange Protocol (SCEP): a lightweight, machine-readable contract and operational workflow that governs temporary capacity borrowing (bursting) between tenant services and the platform during demand spikes. SCEP aligns SLOs, error budgets, and capacity reconcil...
text:
Summary
-------
This document defines the SLO Contract Exchange Protocol (SCEP): a lightweight, machine-readable contract and operational workflow that governs temporary capacity borrowing (bursting) between tenant services and the platform during demand spikes. SCEP aligns SLOs, error budgets, and capacity reconciliation to allow predictable short-term overprovisioning while protecting platform-wide reliability.

Scope
-----
- Applies to all multi-tenant inference workloads served by Redwood’s hosted and dedicated lanes.
- Covers burst events where a tenant requests >20% additional token-serving capacity for up to 72 hours.
- Does not replace long-term capacity reservations or formal Dedicated contracts.

Goals
-----
1) Provide a standard schema for temporary capacity requests tied to explicit SLO concessions.
2) Automate policy evaluation and capacity allocation where possible.
3) R...

title: Failover Controller CLI (rfctl) — Operator Tool Design
preview:
## Overview
This document proposes **rfctl** ("Redwood Failover Controller Tool"), an operator-facing CLI used by on-call and incident responders to:

- Inspect **why** failover automation is (or is not) recommending actions.
- Run **dry-run** / **simulation** evaluations deterministically against current or recorde...
text:
## Overview
This document proposes **rfctl** ("Redwood Failover Controller Tool"), an operator-facing CLI used by on-call and incident responders to:

- Inspect **why** failover automation is (or is not) recommending actions.
- Run **dry-run** / **simulation** evaluations deterministically against current or recorded signals.
- Execute or approve **guardrailed failover actions** (when configured to require human acknowledgement).
- Generate a consistent, structured **audit trail** for all actions and recommendations (including no-ops).

rfctl is intentionally designed as a thin client over existing services:
- Failover Controller (automation)
- Routing Policy Engine (deterministic evaluation)
- Region Health Service (signal aggregation)
- Audit Event Sink (centralized) + Incident Bot (notifications)

This tool is part of the Regional Failover Automation Upgrade under the Reliability P...
